choices: Simply pay a late renewal penalty fee
Complete the renewal form with a supervisor's signature
Submit a full NEW application — renewal is no longer available
Complete 8 additional hours of training only
security.exp: If a registration expires 6 or more months, the guard must submit a FULL NEW APPLICATION — the renewal process is no longer available. This is a major compliance risk; guards should track their expiration date carefully.

security.q: After completing a security guard training course, the school must issue:
choices: A letter of completion on company letterhead
A DCJS-approved certificate of completion
An employer verification form
A Department of State credential
security.exp: Schools must issue 'a DCJS-approved certificate of completion' to each student. Schools also submit training records electronically to DCJS, which transfers them to DOS to support the guard's registration and renewal records.

security.q: The renewal form for an ARMED security guard in New York is:
choices: DOS-2012 (same as unarmed guards)
DOS-1246 (Security Guard Renewal — Armed)
DCJS-47A (Firearms Renewal)
DOS-1473-f (Change Notice)
security.exp: Armed security guard renewal uses DOS-1246 (Security Guard Renewal — Armed). Unarmed guards use DOS-2012. For name/address changes use DOS-1473-f. Each form type serves a distinct purpose — using the wrong form will delay processing.

security.q: For information about whether a prior criminal record affects eligibility for a security guard registration, an applicant should contact:
choices: Their employer's HR department
The DCJS Office of Public Safety
The NYS Department of State at (518) 474-7569
Local law enforcement for an official background check
security.exp: Applicants with criminal records should contact the NYS Department of State, Division of Licensing Services at (518) 474-7569 for a case-specific determination. Eligibility depends on the nature, severity, and timing of the offense — each case is evaluated individually.

security.q: A 'Student Advisory Notice' issued by security guard training schools is:
choices: A warning to students who are failing the training course
A government-required document outlining the training program's conditions that must be provided to all students
A marketing document schools use to recruit students
A government permit allowing the school to operate
security.exp: DCJS-approved training schools must provide every student with a Student Advisory Notice — a required document outlining the conditions and terms of the training program. This requirement protects students from misleading promises about job placement or program content.

security.q: Training records are transferred from DCJS to the Department of State:
choices: By the individual guard submitting copies to both agencies
Automatically and electronically — training schools submit to DCJS which transfers to DOS
Only when the guard formally requests the transfer in writing
At the time of the guard's renewal — not before
security.exp: Training schools submit completion records to DCJS electronically. DCJS then transfers those records to the Department of State. Guards should verify their records appear in the system approximately 3 weeks after completing training.

civics.q: What is the supreme law of the land?
choices: the Bill of Rights
the Declaration of Independence
the Federalist Papers
the Constitution
civics.exp: The U.S. Constitution is the supreme law of the land. All other laws must be consistent with it.

civics.q: What does the Constitution do?
choices: creates the Federal Reserve
establishes the U.S. military
sets up the government and protects basic rights of Americans
declares independence from Britain
civics.exp: The Constitution sets up the structure of the government, defines its powers, and protects the basic rights of Americans.

civics.q: The idea of self-government is in the first three words of the Constitution. What are these words?
choices: In order to
All men are
Congress shall make
We the People
civics.exp: 'We the People' opens the Constitution's Preamble, establishing that the government's power comes from its citizens.

civics.q: What is an amendment?
choices: a presidential order
a Supreme Court ruling
a change or addition to the Constitution
a congressional budget
civics.exp: An amendment is a formal change or addition to the Constitution. There are 27 amendments.

civics.q: What do we call the first ten amendments to the Constitution?
choices: the Articles of Confederation
the Federalist Papers
the Preamble
the Bill of Rights
civics.exp: The first ten amendments are called the Bill of Rights. They protect individual freedoms like speech, religion, and the right to a fair trial.

civics.q: What is one right or freedom from the First Amendment?
choices: the right to bear arms
protection from unreasonable searches
the right to a jury trial
freedom of speech
civics.exp: The First Amendment protects five freedoms: speech, religion, press, assembly, and the right to petition the government.

civics.q: How many amendments does the Constitution have?
choices: thirteen (13)
ten (10)
twenty-seven (27)
fifty (50)
civics.exp: The Constitution has been amended 27 times. The first 10 amendments (1791) are the Bill of Rights; the most recent (1992) limits congressional pay raises.

civics.q: What did the Declaration of Independence do?
choices: created the U.S. government
established the Bill of Rights
announced our independence from Great Britain
abolished slavery
civics.exp: The Declaration of Independence (1776) announced that the 13 colonies were separating from Great Britain and becoming a free nation.

civics.q: What are two rights in the Declaration of Independence?
choices: life and liberty
the right to vote and bear arms
freedom of speech and religion
education and healthcare
civics.exp: The Declaration states that all people are endowed with unalienable rights including life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.

civics.q: What is freedom of religion?
choices: Only Christian religions are permitted
Religion is banned from public spaces
You can practice any religion, or not practice a religion.
The government chooses the national religion
civics.exp: Freedom of religion means the government cannot establish an official religion or prohibit the free exercise of religion.

civics.q: What is the economic system in the United States?
choices: communist economy
socialist economy
feudal economy
capitalist economy
civics.exp: The U.S. has a capitalist (or market) economy, meaning businesses are privately owned and compete in open markets.

civics.q: What is the 'rule of law'?
choices: Congress alone interprets all laws
Laws apply only to citizens, not visitors
The President can override any law
No one is above the law — everyone must follow it, including the government.
civics.exp: The rule of law means that all people and institutions, including the government, are subject to and accountable to the law.

civics.q: Name one branch or part of the government.
choices: the Department of Defense
the Supreme Court building
Congress (legislative branch)
the Federal Reserve
civics.exp: The U.S. government has three branches: legislative (Congress), executive (President), and judicial (the courts).

civics.q: What stops one branch of government from becoming too powerful?
choices: the National Guard
the Federal Bureau of Investigation
the Electoral College
checks and balances
civics.exp: The system of checks and balances gives each branch power to limit the others, preventing any one branch from becoming too powerful.

civics.q: Who is in charge of the executive branch?
choices: the President
the Speaker of the House
the Senate Majority Leader
the Chief Justice
civics.exp: The President of the United States heads the executive branch, which enforces federal laws.

civics.q: Who makes federal laws?
choices: the Cabinet
Congress
the Supreme Court
the President
civics.exp: Congress — the Senate and the House of Representatives — is responsible for making federal laws.

civics.q: What are the two parts of the U.S. Congress?
choices: the Supreme Court and the Cabinet
the House and the Cabinet
the Senate and the Supreme Court
the Senate and the House of Representatives
civics.exp: The U.S. Congress is bicameral, consisting of the Senate (100 senators) and the House of Representatives (435 members).

civics.q: How many U.S. Senators are there?
choices: fifty (50)
nine (9)
one hundred (100)
four hundred thirty-five (435)
civics.exp: There are 100 U.S. Senators — two from each of the 50 states — serving staggered 6-year terms.

civics.q: We elect a U.S. Senator for how many years?
choices: eight (8)
four (4)
two (2)
six (6)
civics.exp: U.S. Senators serve 6-year terms. Elections are staggered so roughly one-third of Senate seats are up for election every two years.

civics.q: Who is one of your state's U.S. Senators now?
choices: Answers will vary by state (e.g., NY: Chuck Schumer or Kirsten Gillibrand)
The President appoints all Senators
Senators are chosen by state governors
There are no Senators — only Representatives
civics.exp: Each state has two U.S. Senators, directly elected by voters. Senators represent all people of the state.

civics.q: The House of Representatives has how many voting members?
choices: four hundred thirty-five (435)
two hundred (200)
five hundred (500)
one hundred (100)
civics.exp: The House has 435 voting members. The number of representatives per state is based on population, determined by the census every 10 years.

civics.q: We elect a U.S. Representative for how many years?
choices: two (2)
eight (8)
six (6)
four (4)
civics.exp: U.S. Representatives serve 2-year terms, meaning the entire House is up for election every two years (midterm and presidential election years).

civics.q: Name your U.S. Representative.
choices: Representatives are appointed, not elected
The President appoints all Representatives
Only states with large populations have Representatives
Answers will vary by congressional district
civics.exp: Each congressional district elects one Representative. You can find yours at www.house.gov using your zip code.

civics.q: Who does a U.S. Senator represent?
choices: only citizens born in the state
only people who voted for them
all people of the state
only registered voters of the state
civics.exp: A U.S. Senator represents all people in their state — voters, non-voters, and non-citizens alike.

civics.q: Why do some states have more Representatives than other states?
choices: because of the state's age of statehood
because of the state's population
because of the state's geographic size
because of the state's economic output
civics.exp: House seats are apportioned by population. California (largest population) has 52 representatives; Wyoming (smallest) has 1.

civics.q: We elect a President for how many years?
choices: two (2)
eight (8)
four (4)
six (6)
civics.exp: Presidents serve 4-year terms. The 22nd Amendment limits presidents to two terms (eight years maximum).

civics.q: In what month do we vote for President?
choices: January
September
October
November
civics.exp: Presidential elections are held on the first Tuesday after the first Monday in November, every four years.

civics.q: What is the name of the President of the United States now?
choices: George Washington
John Adams
Answers will vary — the current president at time of your interview
Abraham Lincoln
civics.exp: You must know who is currently serving as President. The USCIS officer will not accept an outdated answer.

civics.q: What is the name of the Vice President of the United States now?
choices: There is currently no Vice President
The Vice President is always from the opposing party
Answers will vary — the current vice president at time of your interview
The Vice President is appointed by Congress
civics.exp: The Vice President is elected on the same ticket as the President and serves as President of the Senate.

civics.q: If the President can no longer serve, who becomes President?
choices: the Vice President
the Speaker of the House
the Secretary of State
the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court
civics.exp: The Vice President is first in the presidential line of succession. The 25th Amendment establishes this process.

civics.q: If both the President and the Vice President can no longer serve, who becomes President?
choices: the Senate Majority Leader
the Speaker of the House
the Chief Justice
the Secretary of State
civics.exp: Under the Presidential Succession Act, the Speaker of the House is third in line after the Vice President.

civics.q: Who is the Commander in Chief of the military?
choices: the President
Congress
the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
the Secretary of Defense
civics.exp: Article II of the Constitution designates the President as Commander in Chief of the Army, Navy, and all military forces.

civics.q: Who signs bills to become laws?
choices: the President
the Vice President
the Speaker of the House
the Chief Justice
civics.exp: After Congress passes a bill, it goes to the President. The President's signature makes it law; a veto sends it back to Congress.

civics.q: Who vetoes bills?
choices: the Supreme Court
the Secretary of State
the President
the Senate Majority Leader
civics.exp: The President can veto (reject) a bill passed by Congress. Congress can override a veto with a two-thirds vote in both chambers.

civics.q: What does the President's Cabinet do?
choices: passes federal laws
interprets the Constitution
advises the President
commands the military
civics.exp: The Cabinet consists of the Vice President and heads of 15 executive departments (like State, Defense, Treasury). They advise the President.

civics.q: What are two Cabinet-level positions?
choices: Chief Justice and Attorney General
Speaker of the House and Senate Majority Leader
Director of the FBI and CIA Director
Secretary of State and Secretary of Defense
civics.exp: Cabinet positions include Secretaries of State, Defense, Treasury, Education, and others. The Vice President is also Cabinet-level.

civics.q: What does the judicial branch do?
choices: makes federal laws
reviews laws and explains the meaning of the Constitution
enforces federal laws
commands the military
civics.exp: The judicial branch — led by the Supreme Court — interprets laws and determines if they are constitutional.

civics.q: What is the highest court in the United States?
choices: the Supreme Court
the Federal District Court
the Court of Appeals
the International Court of Justice
civics.exp: The Supreme Court is the highest court in the U.S. It has final authority on matters of federal law and the Constitution.

civics.q: How many justices are on the Supreme Court?
choices: seven (7)
nine (9)
fifteen (15)
twelve (12)
civics.exp: There are 9 Supreme Court justices — one Chief Justice and eight Associate Justices. They are appointed by the President and confirmed by the Senate.

civics.q: Who is the Chief Justice of the United States now?
choices: The position rotates among justices
The Chief Justice is elected by the public
The Chief Justice is appointed by Congress
Answers will vary — the current Chief Justice at time of your interview
civics.exp: The Chief Justice is appointed by the President and confirmed by the Senate. John Roberts has served since 2005.

civics.q: Under our Constitution, some powers belong to the federal government. What is one power of the federal government?
choices: to provide schooling
to issue driver's licenses
to print money
to set up local governments
civics.exp: Federal powers include printing money, declaring war, creating treaties, and regulating interstate commerce.

civics.q: Under our Constitution, some powers belong to the states. What is one power of the states?
choices: declare war
print money
provide schooling and education
create an army
civics.exp: State powers include providing education, issuing driver's licenses, approving zoning laws, and providing safety through local police.

civics.q: Who is the Governor of your state now?
choices: Congress appoints state governors
Governors are appointed by the President
All states share one Governor
Answers will vary by state
civics.exp: Each state has its own Governor, elected by the state's voters. You should know the name of your current Governor.

civics.q: What is the capital of your state?
choices: States do not have separate capitals
Answers will vary by state
Washington D.C. is every state's capital
The capital is always the largest city
civics.exp: Each state has its own capital city. For New York, the capital is Albany (not New York City).

civics.q: What are the two major political parties in the United States?
choices: Liberal and Conservative
Democratic and Republican
Whig and Tory
Federalist and Anti-Federalist
civics.exp: The two major parties are the Democratic Party (founded 1828) and the Republican Party (founded 1854). Third parties also exist.

civics.q: What is the political party of the President now?
choices: The President represents all parties equally
Answers will vary — the party of the current president
Political parties are banned for Presidents
The President must be independent
civics.exp: You must know the current President's political party for your naturalization interview.

civics.q: What is the name of the Speaker of the House of Representatives now?
choices: The Speaker is always from New York
The Speaker is appointed by the President
The Senate elects the Speaker
Answers will vary — the current Speaker at time of your interview
civics.exp: The Speaker of the House is elected by House members and is third in the presidential line of succession.

civics.q: There are four amendments to the Constitution about who can vote. Describe one of them.
choices: Only land-owning citizens can vote
Only people who pay taxes can vote
Citizens eighteen (18) and older can vote.
Only citizens born in the U.S. can vote
civics.exp: The 15th, 19th, 24th, and 26th Amendments expanded voting rights to Black Americans, women, non-taxpayers, and citizens 18+.

civics.q: What is one responsibility that is only for United States citizens?
choices: respect the rights of others
serve on a jury
pay taxes
obey the law
civics.exp: Only U.S. citizens can serve on a jury or vote in federal elections. Paying taxes and obeying the law apply to everyone.

civics.q: Name one right only for United States citizens.
choices: freedom of speech
freedom of religion
the right to a fair trial
vote in a federal election
civics.exp: Voting in federal elections is a right reserved for U.S. citizens. Most other constitutional rights apply to all people in the U.S.

civics.q: What are two rights of everyone living in the United States?
choices: citizenship and a U.S. passport
the right to vote and hold public office
the right to work and collect Social Security
freedom of expression and freedom of speech
civics.exp: Constitutional rights like freedom of speech, religion, and the right to a fair trial apply to everyone in the U.S., not just citizens.

civics.q: What do we show loyalty to when we say the Pledge of Allegiance?
choices: the President and Congress
the Supreme Court and the law
the Constitution and the military
the United States and the flag
civics.exp: The Pledge of Allegiance is a promise of loyalty to the U.S. flag and the republic for which it stands.

civics.q: What is one promise you make when you become a United States citizen?
choices: give up loyalty to other countries
serve in the military for 4 years
live in the U.S. for at least 10 years
learn to speak English fluently within 1 year
civics.exp: The Oath of Allegiance requires new citizens to renounce foreign allegiances, support the Constitution, and defend the U.S.

civics.q: How old do citizens have to be to vote for President?
choices: eighteen (18) and older
twenty-one (21) and older
twenty-five (25) and older
sixteen (16) and older
civics.exp: The 26th Amendment (1971) lowered the voting age from 21 to 18. All citizens 18 and older may vote.

civics.q: What are two ways that Americans can participate in their democracy?
choices: travel abroad and learn other languages
vote and join a political party
work for the government and attend school
pay taxes and serve in the military
civics.exp: Americans participate in democracy by voting, running for office, joining political parties, helping with campaigns, and contacting representatives.

civics.q: When is the last day you can send in federal income tax forms?
choices: April 15
December 31
January 1
July 4
civics.exp: Federal income tax returns are due on April 15 each year (or the next business day if it falls on a weekend or holiday).

civics.q: When must all men register for the Selective Service?
choices: at age eighteen (18)
at age sixteen (16)
when they become a citizen
only during wartime
civics.exp: All male residents (citizens and immigrants) between 18–25 must register with the Selective Service System, which maintains a list for potential military draft.

civics.q: What is one reason colonists came to America?
choices: to find gold and silver only
to escape the Great Famine
to avoid paying European taxes only
freedom
civics.exp: Colonists came to America for freedom (political, religious, and economic), to escape persecution, and to find opportunities unavailable in Europe.

civics.q: Who lived in America before the Europeans arrived?
choices: Asian immigrants
Spanish conquistadors
African Americans
American Indians
civics.exp: American Indians (also called Native Americans) lived in North America for thousands of years before European contact.

civics.q: What group of people was taken to America and sold as slaves?
choices: Asian indentured servants
Africans
Europeans from Ireland
Native Americans only
civics.exp: Millions of Africans were forcibly brought to America and sold into slavery from the 1600s through the Civil War.

civics.q: Why did the colonists fight the British?
choices: because Britain refused to trade with the colonies
because the colonists wanted to expand westward
because Britain invaded the colonies
because of high taxes (taxation without representation)
civics.exp: Colonists resisted 'taxation without representation' — being taxed by a British Parliament in which they had no elected voice.

civics.q: Who wrote the Declaration of Independence?
choices: John Adams
Benjamin Franklin
Thomas Jefferson
George Washington
civics.exp: Thomas Jefferson was the principal author of the Declaration of Independence (1776), with edits from the Continental Congress.

civics.q: When was the Declaration of Independence adopted?
choices: July 4, 1789
March 4, 1781
July 4, 1776
September 17, 1787
civics.exp: The Declaration of Independence was formally adopted by the Continental Congress on July 4, 1776, which is why we celebrate Independence Day on that date.

civics.q: There were 13 original states. Name three.
choices: New York, Virginia, and Massachusetts
Florida, Texas, and California
Colorado, Utah, and Nevada
Ohio, Michigan, and Illinois
civics.exp: The 13 original states were the former British colonies: CT, DE, GA, MD, MA, NH, NJ, NY, NC, PA, RI, SC, and VA.

civics.q: What happened at the Constitutional Convention?
choices: The Bill of Rights was passed
The Constitution was written.
The Revolutionary War ended
The Declaration of Independence was signed
civics.exp: The Constitutional Convention met in Philadelphia in 1787. Delegates drafted the U.S. Constitution, which replaced the weaker Articles of Confederation.

civics.q: When was the Constitution written?
civics.exp: The Constitution was drafted at the Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia in 1787 and ratified in 1788. The Bill of Rights was added in 1791.

civics.q: The Federalist Papers supported the passage of the U.S. Constitution. Name one of the writers.
choices: Thomas Jefferson
George Washington
James Madison
Patrick Henry
civics.exp: The Federalist Papers (85 essays) were written by James Madison, Alexander Hamilton, and John Jay under the pen name 'Publius' to advocate ratification of the Constitution.

civics.q: What is one thing Benjamin Franklin is famous for?
choices: serving as the first President
writing the Constitution
commanding the Continental Army
U.S. diplomat and Founding Father
civics.exp: Benjamin Franklin was a diplomat, inventor (lightning rod, bifocals), writer (Poor Richard's Almanack), and Founding Father who helped draft the Declaration of Independence.

civics.q: Who is the 'Father of Our Country'?
choices: George Washington
John Adams
Thomas Jefferson
Benjamin Franklin
civics.exp: George Washington is called the 'Father of Our Country.' He commanded the Continental Army, presided over the Constitutional Convention, and was the first President.

civics.q: Who was the first President?
choices: John Adams
George Washington
Thomas Jefferson
Benjamin Franklin
civics.exp: George Washington was unanimously elected the first President of the United States in 1789 and served two terms until 1797.

civics.q: What territory did the United States buy from France in 1803?
choices: the Texas Territory
the Florida Territory
the Oregon Territory
the Louisiana Territory
civics.exp: The Louisiana Purchase (1803) doubled the size of the U.S. President Jefferson bought approximately 828,000 square miles from France for $15 million.

civics.q: Name one war fought by the United States in the 1800s.
choices: World War I
the Vietnam War
the Civil War
the Korean War
civics.exp: Wars in the 1800s include the War of 1812, the Mexican-American War (1846–48), the Civil War (1861–65), and the Spanish-American War (1898).

civics.q: Name the U.S. war between the North and the South.
choices: the War of 1812
the Spanish-American War
the Civil War
the Revolutionary War
civics.exp: The Civil War (1861–65) was fought between the Union (North) and the Confederacy (South), primarily over slavery and states' rights.

civics.q: Name one problem that led to the Civil War.
choices: disagreements over foreign trade
slavery
conflicts with Native American tribes
immigration from Europe
civics.exp: The Civil War's main causes included slavery, states' rights, and economic differences between the industrial North and agricultural South.

civics.q: What was one important thing that Abraham Lincoln did?
choices: purchased the Louisiana Territory
freed the slaves (Emancipation Proclamation)
wrote the Declaration of Independence
led the Continental Army
civics.exp: Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation (1863) freeing enslaved people in Confederate states and preserved the Union by winning the Civil War.

civics.q: What did the Emancipation Proclamation do?
choices: gave women the right to vote
established the Supreme Court
ended the Civil War
freed the slaves in Confederate states
civics.exp: The Emancipation Proclamation (January 1, 1863) declared that all enslaved people in Confederate states 'shall be then, thenceforward, and forever free.'

civics.q: What did Susan B. Anthony do?
choices: wrote the Emancipation Proclamation
founded the Red Cross
led the Underground Railroad
fought for women's rights and suffrage
civics.exp: Susan B. Anthony (1820–1906) was a leading suffragist who fought for women's right to vote. The 19th Amendment (1920) is often called the Susan B. Anthony Amendment.

civics.q: Name one war fought by the United States in the 1900s.
choices: the Civil War
the Revolutionary War
the War of 1812
World War II
civics.exp: U.S. wars in the 1900s include World War I (1917–18), World War II (1941–45), the Korean War (1950–53), and the Vietnam War (1964–75).

civics.q: Who was President during World War I?
choices: Woodrow Wilson
Theodore Roosevelt
Franklin D. Roosevelt
William Howard Taft
civics.exp: Woodrow Wilson was President when the U.S. entered World War I in 1917. He proposed the League of Nations in his Fourteen Points.

civics.q: Who was President during the Great Depression and World War II?
choices: Dwight Eisenhower
Franklin Roosevelt
Herbert Hoover
Harry Truman
civics.exp: Franklin D. Roosevelt (FDR) served as President from 1933–1945, leading the country through the Great Depression (New Deal) and most of WWII.

civics.q: Who did the United States fight in World War II?
choices: China, Korea, and Vietnam
Japan, Germany, and Italy
Spain, Portugal, and Austria
France, Britain, and Russia
civics.exp: In WWII, the U.S. fought the Axis Powers: Japan (Pacific Theater), Germany, and Italy (European Theater).

civics.q: Before he was President, Eisenhower was a general. What war was he in?
choices: World War II
the Korean War
World War I
the Vietnam War
civics.exp: Dwight D. Eisenhower commanded Allied forces in Europe during WWII, including the D-Day invasion at Normandy in 1944.

civics.q: During the Cold War, what was the main concern of the United States?
choices: Communism
immigration
terrorism
nuclear power
civics.exp: The Cold War (1947–1991) was a geopolitical tension between the U.S. and the Soviet Union, centered on the spread of Communism vs. democracy.

civics.q: What movement tried to end racial discrimination?
choices: the labor movement
the temperance movement
the civil rights movement
the suffrage movement
civics.exp: The civil rights movement of the 1950s–60s, led by figures like Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., fought to end racial discrimination and segregation.

civics.q: What did Martin Luther King, Jr. do?
choices: founded the NAACP
signed the Emancipation Proclamation
fought for civil rights
led the Union Army in the Civil War
civics.exp: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was the foremost leader of the civil rights movement. He advocated nonviolent protest and delivered the famous 'I Have a Dream' speech (1963).

civics.q: What major event happened on September 11, 2001, in the United States?
choices: A major hurricane destroyed New Orleans
The stock market crashed
Terrorists attacked the United States
The United States invaded Iraq
civics.exp: On September 11, 2001, al-Qaeda terrorists hijacked four airplanes, attacking the World Trade Center in NYC, the Pentagon, and crashing one in Pennsylvania.

civics.q: Name one American Indian tribe in the United States.
choices: Aztec
Cherokee
Inca
Maya
civics.exp: American Indian tribes include the Cherokee, Navajo, Sioux, Chippewa, Choctaw, Apache, Iroquois, and many others.

civics.q: Name one of the two longest rivers in the United States.
choices: the Ohio River
the Rio Grande
the Colorado River
the Missouri River
civics.exp: The two longest U.S. rivers are the Missouri River (2,341 miles) and the Mississippi River (2,340 miles). The Mississippi is more historically significant.

civics.q: What ocean is on the West Coast of the United States?
choices: the Arctic Ocean
the Indian Ocean
the Pacific Ocean
the Atlantic Ocean
civics.exp: The Pacific Ocean borders the U.S. West Coast (California, Oregon, Washington, Alaska, Hawaii). The Atlantic Ocean borders the East Coast.

civics.q: What ocean is on the East Coast of the United States?
choices: the Gulf of Mexico
the Atlantic Ocean
the Arctic Ocean
the Pacific Ocean
civics.exp: The Atlantic Ocean borders the U.S. East Coast from Maine to Florida. The Gulf of Mexico borders the southern coast.

civics.q: Name one U.S. territory.
choices: Greenland
Puerto Rico
Cuba
Bermuda
civics.exp: U.S. territories include Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands, American Samoa, Northern Mariana Islands, and Guam.

civics.q: Name one state that borders Canada.
choices: Texas
California
Florida
New York
civics.exp: States bordering Canada include Maine, New Hampshire, Vermont, New York, Pennsylvania, Ohio, Michigan, Minnesota, North Dakota, Montana, Idaho, Washington, and Alaska.

civics.q: Name one state that borders Mexico.
choices: Arkansas
Florida
California
Louisiana
civics.exp: The four states bordering Mexico are California, Arizona, New Mexico, and Texas.

civics.q: What is the capital of the United States?
choices: Washington, D.C.
Philadelphia
Boston
New York City
civics.exp: Washington, D.C. (District of Columbia) has been the capital of the United States since 1800. It is not part of any state.

civics.q: Where is the Statue of Liberty?
choices: New York (Harbor) / Liberty Island
Boston, Massachusetts
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Washington, D.C.
civics.exp: The Statue of Liberty stands on Liberty Island in New York Harbor, a gift from France dedicated in 1886. It symbolizes freedom and democracy.

civics.q: Why does the flag have 13 stripes?
choices: to represent the 13 amendments in the Bill of Rights
because there were 13 original colonies
because there are 13 original states today
to represent the 13 months of the first calendar
civics.exp: The 13 alternating red and white stripes represent the 13 original British colonies that became the first states.

civics.q: Why does the flag have 50 stars?
choices: because the flag was designed in 1950
because each star represents 10 million people
because there are 50 senators
because there are 50 states
civics.exp: The 50 stars on the U.S. flag represent the 50 states. Hawaii was the last state admitted (1960), bringing the star count to 50.

civics.q: What is the name of the national anthem?
choices: America the Beautiful
God Bless America
My Country 'Tis of Thee
The Star-Spangled Banner
civics.exp: 'The Star-Spangled Banner' was written by Francis Scott Key in 1814 and officially became the national anthem in 1931.

civics.q: When do we celebrate Independence Day?
choices: November 11
July 14
July 4
September 4
civics.exp: Independence Day (July 4) celebrates the adoption of the Declaration of Independence on July 4, 1776.

civics.q: Name two national U.S. holidays.
choices: Easter and Halloween
Christmas and Hanukkah
Valentine's Day and St. Patrick's Day
Independence Day and Thanksgiving
civics.exp: Federal holidays include New Year's Day, MLK Day, Presidents' Day, Memorial Day, Juneteenth, Independence Day, Labor Day, Columbus Day, Veterans Day, Thanksgiving, and Christmas.

food_protection.q: Under Article 81 of the NYC Health Code, which office performs food safety inspections?
choices: Bureau of Fire Safety
Bureau of Food Safety and Community Sanitation
Department of Buildings
Consumer Affairs Division
food_protection.exp: The Bureau of Food Safety and Community Sanitation, within the Division of Environmental Health, is responsible for inspecting food service establishments under Article 81 of the NYC Health Code.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Health Code & Introduction

food_protection.q: Who is required by the NYC Health Code to hold a Food Protection Certificate?
choices: Every food service employee
The owner of every restaurant
Supervisors of food service and non-retail food processing establishments
Only catering managers
food_protection.exp: NYC Health Code requires supervisors of food service establishments and non-retail food processing establishments to hold a Food Protection Certificate. At least one certified supervisor must be on premises at all times.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Health Code & Introduction

food_protection.q: A Sanitarian (Health Inspector) shows up at your restaurant. Which of the following is TRUE?
choices: Inspectors must schedule visits 48 hours in advance
Inspectors have the right to inspect any area of the establishment at any time it is in operation
Inspectors can collect fines on behalf of the Department
Inspectors are authorized to conduct monetary transactions
food_protection.exp: Health Inspectors have the right to inspect any and all areas of a food establishment at any time it is in operation. They are NOT authorized to collect fines or conduct monetary transactions.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Health Code & Introduction

food_protection.q: Offering a bribe or gratuity to a Sanitarian is:
choices: Acceptable if the amount is small
A minor violation
A crime that can result in fines and imprisonment
Standard industry practice
food_protection.exp: It is illegal to offer a Sanitarian any bribe, gratuity, or reward for official misconduct. This is a criminal offense that can result in fines, imprisonment, and revocation of permits.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Health Code & Introduction

food_protection.q: The Temperature Danger Zone for bacterial growth is:
choices: 0°F to 41°F
food_protection.exp: The Temperature Danger Zone is 41°F to 140°F. Within this range, bacteria grow and multiply rapidly. Food must be kept either below 41°F (cold) or above 140°F (hot) to remain safe.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Safety Fundamentals

food_protection.q: Which of the following is classified as a Potentially Hazardous Food (PHF)?
choices: Crackers
Honey
Cooked rice
Dried fruit
food_protection.exp: PHFs are foods high in protein or starch that support rapid bacterial growth: raw or cooked meats, poultry, fish, eggs, dairy, cooked rice/pasta/potatoes, tofu. Crackers, honey, and dried fruit have low water activity and are exceptions.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Safety Fundamentals

food_protection.q: What is the water activity (Aw) value below which bacteria cannot grow rapidly?
food_protection.exp: Foods with a water activity of 0.85 or less do not support the rapid growth of bacteria. This is why dried foods and honey are safer — they have very low water activity.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Safety Fundamentals

food_protection.q: A food-borne illness OUTBREAK is defined as:
choices: One person getting sick from a food
Any complaint about food quality
Two or more persons becoming ill with similar symptoms from the same source
A critical violation found by a health inspector
food_protection.exp: A foodborne illness outbreak is defined as any incident involving two or more persons who become ill with similar symptoms traced to the same food source.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Safety Fundamentals

food_protection.q: Which populations are MOST vulnerable to severe foodborne illness?
choices: Teenagers and young adults
Children with undeveloped immune systems, elderly, pregnant women, and immunocompromised individuals
Professional athletes
Adults between ages 30–50
food_protection.exp: High-risk populations include children (undeveloped immune system), elderly (weakened immunity), pregnant women (risk to fetus), and immunocompromised individuals (AIDS, cancer, diabetes, medications).
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Safety Fundamentals

food_protection.q: The MOST common causes of foodborne illness include all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: Sick food worker
Improper holding temperatures
Using fresh herbs as garnish
Cross contamination
food_protection.exp: Using fresh herbs as a garnish is not a common cause of foodborne illness. The most common causes are: sick food workers, poor personal hygiene, improper temperatures, inadequate cooking/cooling, and cross contamination.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Safety Fundamentals

food_protection.q: Fresh beef must be received at what temperature?
choices: 50°F or below
45°F or below
41°F or below
32°F or below
food_protection.exp: Fresh beef must be received at 41°F or below. Frozen beef must be at 0°F or below. The USDA inspection stamp (not grade stamp) is the more important stamp to verify.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Receiving Foods

food_protection.q: Chicken and poultry must be received at:
choices: 45°F or below
41°F fresh, 0°F frozen
50°F fresh, 32°F frozen
38°F or below
food_protection.exp: Chicken and poultry must be received at 41°F or below when fresh, and at 0°F or less when frozen. Poultry is naturally contaminated with Salmonella and must be handled carefully.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Receiving Foods

food_protection.q: Shellfish tags must be kept with the product and then filed in order of delivery. How long must they be kept after the last product is used?
choices: 30 days
60 days
90 days
1 year
food_protection.exp: Shellfish tags must be kept together with the product until all of it is used, and then filed in order of delivery and kept for 90 days. This is required for traceability.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Receiving Foods

food_protection.q: An opened shell indicates that shellfish may be dead. To check if a shellfish is alive, you should:
choices: Smell it — live shellfish have no odor
Gently tap the shell — if it closes, it is alive
Press the shell — if it's firm, it's alive
Place it in water — if it floats, it's alive
food_protection.exp: Gently tap the shell of a bivalve (clam, mussel, oyster). If the shell closes, the shellfish is alive. An open shell that doesn't close when tapped indicates a dead shellfish that must be discarded.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Receiving Foods

food_protection.q: Fresh shell eggs must be refrigerated at an ambient temperature of:
choices: 41°F or below
45°F or below
50°F or below
38°F or below
food_protection.exp: Fresh shell eggs must be kept refrigerated at an ambient temperature of 45°F or below (note: storage temperature differs from other PHFs). Eggs carry Salmonella enteritidis internally — washing does not make them safe.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Receiving Foods

food_protection.q: Which of the following canned goods must be REMOVED from circulation?
choices: A can with slight surface rust only
A can that is concave on both ends
A can with a dent on one of the three seams (top, bottom, or side)
A can that has been on the shelf for 3 months
food_protection.exp: Cans with dents on any of the three seams (top, bottom, side) must be removed. Severely rusted, severely dented, leaking, or swollen cans are also rejected. Slight rust or minor dents away from seams are minor defects.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Receiving Foods

food_protection.q: Smoked fish provides ideal conditions for the growth of Clostridium botulinum spores and must be stored at:
choices: 41°F or below
38°F or below
32°F or below
45°F or below
food_protection.exp: Smoked fish must be stored at 38°F or below per NYC Health Code requirements due to the botulinum risk. This is a stricter requirement than standard refrigerated PHF storage.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Receiving Foods

food_protection.q: Pasteurized milk and milk products must not exceed how many days from the date of pasteurization before the expiration date?
choices: Seven days
Nine calendar days
Fourteen days
Twenty-one days
food_protection.exp: Pasteurized milk expiration must not exceed nine calendar days from pasteurization. Ultra-pasteurized milk products must not exceed 45 days. Upon receipt, verify these dates and ensure storage at 41°F.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Receiving Foods

food_protection.q: Signs of SPOILED fresh fish include all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: Cloudy, sunken eyes
Strong fishy odor
Bright red gills
Flesh that pits under pressure
food_protection.exp: Bright red gills are a sign of FRESH fish. Signs of spoilage: cloudy/sunken eyes, grey/greenish gills, strong fishy odor, flesh that pits or pulls away from bones, loose scales.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Receiving Foods

food_protection.q: What does FIFO stand for in food storage?
choices: Food In, Food Out
First Inspection, Final Order
First In, First Out
Freeze If Food Overheats
food_protection.exp: FIFO = First In, First Out. Older stock is moved to the front and used first; newer stock goes to the back. The first step in FIFO is to date all products when received.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Storage

food_protection.q: Per NYC Health Code, food must be stored at least how many inches off the floor?
choices: 2 inches
4 inches
6 inches
12 inches
food_protection.exp: All food must be stored at least 6 inches (15 cm) off the floor. This allows for proper cleaning and also allows detection of vermin activity.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Storage

food_protection.q: In a refrigerator, raw foods must be stored _____ cooked foods to prevent cross contamination.
choices: Above
Beside
Below
It doesn't matter
food_protection.exp: Raw foods (meat, poultry, seafood) must always be stored BELOW cooked and ready-to-eat foods in a refrigerator. Raw juices can drip down and contaminate ready-to-eat foods.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Storage

food_protection.q: Cold temperature does NOT kill microorganisms; it:
choices: Eliminates bacteria completely over time
Slows down their growth
Kills bacteria within 24 hours
Destroys bacterial spores
food_protection.exp: Cold temperature slows the growth of microorganisms but does NOT kill them. When food warms up, bacteria resume normal growth. This is why proper cooking temperatures are still required.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Storage

food_protection.q: Which food storage container material is PROHIBITED for storing acidic foods?
choices: Stainless steel
Food-grade plastic
Copper, brass, tin, and galvanized metal
Glass
food_protection.exp: Copper, brass, tin, and galvanized metal containers react with high-acid foods (sauerkraut, citrus juices, tomato sauce) and cause toxic metal poisoning. Food-grade plastic or stainless steel must be used.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Storage

food_protection.q: When frozen foods show signs of thawing and refreezing (ice crystals on packaging, water stains), they should be:
choices: Accepted if the temperature is currently below 32°F
Thawed out completely and used immediately
Rejected — these are signs of temperature abuse
Stored in a separate freezer section
food_protection.exp: Signs of thawing and refreezing indicate temperature abuse. Once thawed, harmful bacteria can reproduce to dangerous levels. When refrozen, the quality and safety are compromised.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Storage

food_protection.q: Foods stored in ice (as for display) require that:
choices: The ice acts as a container and food can be submerged
Water from melted ice is constantly drained so food is on ice, not in water
Ice can be reused for human consumption after draining
Any type of ice, including machine ice, can be used
food_protection.exp: When storing in ice, water from melted ice must be constantly drained so food stays on ice and is not immersed. It is improper to store food in ice machines or use that ice for human consumption.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Storage

food_protection.q: A physical hazard in food is:
choices: Salmonella bacteria in chicken
Histamine produced by decomposing fish
A piece of glass from a broken light bulb
Norovirus from a sick food worker
food_protection.exp: A physical hazard is a foreign object that can cause injury or illness: glass, metal shavings, bones, toothpicks, stones, jewelry. Chemical and biological hazards are separate categories.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hazards

food_protection.q: MSG (Monosodium Glutamate) in food service:
choices: Is prohibited under NYC Health Code
May be used as long as its use is disclosed on the menu
May be added in any amount without disclosure
Is a required food additive for flavor
food_protection.exp: MSG is permitted in a food service establishment as long as its use is disclosed on the menu. However, sulfites are PROHIBITED — they can cause serious allergic reactions and cannot be added in food service.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hazards

food_protection.q: Scombroid poisoning is caused by:
choices: Eating raw shellfish from polluted waters
Consuming histamine produced by decomposing fish from the Scombridae family (tuna, mackerel, bluefish)
Bacteria growing in undercooked pork
A parasite found in freshwater fish
food_protection.exp: Scombroid poisoning results from histamine produced when fish from the Scombridae family (tuna, mackerel, bonito, mahi mahi, bluefish) decompose. Histamine is NOT destroyed by cooking. Symptoms mimic a heart attack.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hazards

food_protection.q: Ciguatera poisoning is associated with:
choices: Improperly canned vegetables
Predatory fish (barracuda) that accumulate ciguatoxin from algae through the food chain
Undercooked ground beef
Raw shellfish from untagged sources
food_protection.exp: Ciguatoxin accumulates through the marine food chain. Predatory fish like barracuda feed on smaller fish that fed on toxic algae. Ciguatoxin is NOT destroyed by cooking and causes neurological symptoms.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hazards

food_protection.q: Which of the following represents a CHEMICAL hazard in food?
choices: A small pebble found in dried beans
A metal fragment from a worn can opener
Roach spray improperly stored near open food
Mold growing on old bread
food_protection.exp: Roach spray near open food is a chemical hazard. Physical hazards include pebbles and metal fragments. Mold is a biological hazard.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hazards

food_protection.q: The eight most common food allergens (major food allergens) are:
choices: Corn, wheat, rice, oats, soy, milk, eggs, tree nuts
Fish, peanuts, wheat, soy, tree nuts, eggs, milk, shellfish
Shellfish, fish, peanuts, corn, soy, wheat, eggs, sesame
Fish, peanuts, soy, dairy, gluten, tree nuts, eggs, meat
food_protection.exp: The 8 major allergens: Fish, Peanuts, Wheat, Soy, Tree Nuts, Eggs, Milk, Shellfish. Memory device: Food Problems Will Send The EMS. These 8 account for 90% of all allergic reactions.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Allergies

food_protection.q: If a guest has a severe allergic reaction in your establishment, the FIRST action is:
choices: Offer them antihistamine from the first aid kit
Call 911 immediately
Ask them to wait — allergic reactions usually pass
Remove the allergen from their plate
food_protection.exp: If a guest has an allergic reaction, call 911 immediately. Anaphylaxis can cause loss of consciousness and death due to airway obstruction. Do not delay medical help.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Allergies

food_protection.q: Removing an allergen (such as nuts) from a finished dish makes the dish safe for allergic customers.
choices: True — if the allergen is fully removed
True — if hands are washed after
False — even trace amounts can cause serious reactions
False — only if the dish is recooked
food_protection.exp: FALSE. Removing an allergen from a finished dish does NOT make it safe. Even trace amounts of allergen can cause a severe reaction. The dish must be prepared from scratch with allergen-free ingredients.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Allergies

food_protection.q: Sulfites in food service establishments:
choices: Can be added in small amounts
Are permitted if disclosed on the menu
Are PROHIBITED — they cannot be added in a food service establishment
Must be declared on ingredient labels only
food_protection.exp: The use of sulfites is PROHIBITED in food service establishments. Sulfites (used to maintain color and freshness) can cause serious allergic reactions. Some foods may naturally contain sulfites when received, but none may be added.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Allergies

food_protection.q: The six conditions that affect bacterial growth are represented by the acronym FATTOM. What does FATTOM stand for?
choices: Food, Acidity, Temperature, Time, Oxygen, Moisture
Fat, Acidity, Temperature, Toxin, Organisms, Moisture
Food, Air, Temperature, Time, Origin, Mass
Freshness, Acidity, Toxins, Temperature, Organisms, Moisture
food_protection.exp: FATTOM = Food (nutrient source), Acidity (neutral pH preferred), Temperature (41–140°F), Time (>2 hours in danger zone), Oxygen (aerobic/anaerobic), Moisture (water activity). These are the conditions bacteria need to multiply.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microbiology — Bacteria

food_protection.q: Bacteria that grow best at temperatures between 50°F and 110°F are called:
choices: Thermophilic bacteria
Mesophilic bacteria
Psychrophilic bacteria
Pathogenic bacteria
food_protection.exp: Mesophilic bacteria grow best at 50°F–110°F — this range includes normal body temperature and usual room temperature. Most pathogens are mesophilic. Thermophilic prefer 110–150°F; psychrophilic prefer below 50°F.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microbiology — Bacteria

food_protection.q: Under ideal conditions, one bacterium can become two in as little as:
choices: 5–10 minutes
20–30 minutes
1–2 hours
4–6 hours
food_protection.exp: Bacteria reproduce by binary fission (splitting in two). Under ideal conditions, the generation time is 20–30 minutes. Starting from 1,000 organisms, bacterial levels can exceed 1 million within hours.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microbiology — Bacteria

food_protection.q: Bacterial spores are dangerous because:
choices: They multiply more rapidly than regular bacteria
They are resistant to adverse conditions and are difficult to destroy by normal methods
They produce toxins that cause immediate illness
They can only be found in meat products
food_protection.exp: In the spore (non-vegetative) stage, bacteria form a protective shell. Spores are resistant to heat, chemicals, and drying. They don't reproduce but survive harsh conditions and resume growth when conditions improve.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microbiology — Bacteria

food_protection.q: Which type of bacteria does NOT need oxygen to grow?
choices: Aerobic bacteria
Anaerobic bacteria
Mesophilic bacteria
Thermophilic bacteria
food_protection.exp: Anaerobic bacteria grow without oxygen. Aerobic bacteria need oxygen. Facultative anaerobes can grow with or without oxygen. Clostridium botulinum is a dangerous anaerobe — it thrives in vacuum-sealed packages.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microbiology — Bacteria

food_protection.q: Foods with a pH of 4.6 or less are considered:
choices: Ideal for rapid bacterial growth
Too acidic for most bacteria to grow rapidly
Neutral and safe for all bacteria
Too alkaline for bacterial growth
food_protection.exp: A pH of 4.6 or less is too acidic for most pathogenic bacteria to grow rapidly. This is why vinegar is used as a preservative. Foods with pH 4.6 or below (high-acid foods) are relatively safer from bacterial growth.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microbiology — Bacteria

food_protection.q: Unlike bacteria, viruses CANNOT reproduce in food. Food serves as:
choices: A nutrient source for viral growth
A reservoir and transporting mechanism until it is ingested
A way to destroy viruses through heat
A protective environment for viruses
food_protection.exp: Viruses cannot reproduce in food. Food only serves as a reservoir and transport mechanism. Once ingested, viruses use human cells to duplicate. This is why viral contamination from infected food workers is so dangerous.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microorganisms

food_protection.q: The two most common viruses in the food industry are:
choices: Rotavirus and Astrovirus
Hepatitis A and Norovirus (Norwalk virus)
Poliovirus and Influenza
Hepatitis B and Hepatitis C
food_protection.exp: The two most common viral causes of foodborne illness are Hepatitis A (15–50 day onset, causes jaundice) and Norovirus/Norwalk virus (12–48 hour onset, highly contagious, common in cruise ships and institutions).
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microorganisms

food_protection.q: Trichinella spiralis is a parasite associated with:
choices: Raw or undercooked pork and wild game (bear, walrus)
Raw shellfish and marine fish
Unwashed fresh produce
Unpasteurized dairy products
food_protection.exp: Trichinella spiralis causes trichinosis. Pork and pork products, bear, walrus, and other wild game must be cooked to 150°F for at least 15 seconds to destroy this parasite.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microorganisms

food_protection.q: To make fish safe for raw consumption (sushi, sashimi, ceviche), it should be:
choices: Kept fresh on ice at 32°F
Marinated in lemon juice for 2 hours
Frozen at -31°F (-35°C) for 15 hours or below -4°F for 7 days
Soaked in salt water for 24 hours
food_protection.exp: To kill Anisakis and other parasites, fish intended for raw consumption must be frozen at -31°F (-35°C) for 15 hours, or at -4°F (-20°C) for 7 days. Only sushi-grade certified fish meets these requirements.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Microorganisms

food_protection.q: Salmonellosis is MOST commonly associated with:
choices: Home-canned foods and garlic in oil
Animals, poultry, eggs, and humans — especially chicken and eggs
Tuna and mackerel left at room temperature
Raw shellfish from contaminated waters
food_protection.exp: Salmonella is most associated with poultry, eggs, and meat from animals. Control: cook chicken to 165°F, store eggs at 45°F or below, prevent cross contamination.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Foodborne Illnesses

food_protection.q: Staphylococcal gastroenteritis is caused by toxins produced by Staphylococcus aureus. These toxins are:
choices: Destroyed by cooking to 165°F
Destroyed by refrigeration below 41°F
NOT destroyed by heat — cooking cannot eliminate them once formed
Eliminated by chemical sanitizers
food_protection.exp: Staph toxins (once produced) are heat-stable and are NOT destroyed by cooking. This is why prevention focuses on keeping food out of the temperature danger zone and preventing contamination from infected food workers.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Foodborne Illnesses

food_protection.q: Botulism is caused by Clostridium botulinum and is associated with:
choices: Raw poultry and eggs
Home-canned foods, smoked and vacuum-packaged fish, garlic in oil, thick stews
Raw leafy greens and sprouts
Unpasteurized fruit juices
food_protection.exp: C. botulinum thrives in anaerobic (no oxygen) conditions — home-canned foods, vacuum-packaged products, garlic in oil. It produces a toxin that causes neurological symptoms. Home canned foods are NEVER allowed in commercial food establishments.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Foodborne Illnesses

food_protection.q: E. coli O157:H7 (Hemorrhagic colitis) is most associated with:
choices: Chicken and eggs
Raw and undercooked ground beef, unpasteurized milk and produce
Smoked fish and home-canned vegetables
Ready-to-eat foods touched by infected workers
food_protection.exp: E. coli O157:H7 is associated with ground beef (must cook to 158°F), unpasteurized milk, and fresh produce. In children it can cause Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS), responsible for kidney failure.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Foodborne Illnesses

food_protection.q: Listeria monocytogenes is unusual among pathogens because:
choices: It can only survive above 140°F
It cannot be killed by cooking
It can grow at refrigerator temperatures (below 41°F)
It requires no moisture to grow
food_protection.exp: Listeria is particularly dangerous because it can grow at refrigerator temperatures (0–45°F), unlike most pathogens. It is associated with unpasteurized dairy, deli meats, and raw vegetables. Fatal in pregnant women.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Foodborne Illnesses

food_protection.q: Hepatitis A has an onset time of:
choices: 1–6 hours
6–48 hours
2–10 days
15–50 days
food_protection.exp: Hepatitis A has the longest onset time of common foodborne illnesses: 15–50 days. Symptoms include fever, malaise, nausea, abdominal pain, and jaundice. It is associated with raw shellfish, fecal-contaminated water, and infected food workers.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Foodborne Illnesses

food_protection.q: To control Campylobacter jejuni, which is commonly associated with poultry:
choices: Store poultry at 50°F to inhibit growth
Cook poultry to 165°F and prevent cross contamination
Only use irradiated chicken
Marinate chicken in acidic sauce before cooking
food_protection.exp: Campylobacteriosis is controlled by: cooking poultry to 165°F, refrigerating raw poultry at 41°F or below, preventing cross contamination, and practicing good personal hygiene.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Foodborne Illnesses

food_protection.q: A foodborne INTOXICATION (vs. infection) means illness is caused by:
choices: A large number of microorganisms ingested with the food
Toxins produced by microorganisms that are already in the food when eaten
A parasite found in the intestinal tract
A virus that cannot reproduce in food
food_protection.exp: Foodborne intoxication: illness from toxins present in the food (produced before eating). Examples: Staph aureus, Botulism, Scombroid. The organism itself may not be present — just its toxic waste products.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Foodborne Illnesses

food_protection.q: The NYC Health Code PROHIBITS food workers from handling ready-to-eat foods with:
choices: Disposable gloves
Clean metal tongs
Bare hands
Deli paper or tissue
food_protection.exp: NYC Health Code prohibits handling ready-to-eat foods (salads, sandwiches, etc.) with bare hands. Acceptable alternatives: gloves, tongs, deli paper, tissue, or other utensils.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Personal Hygiene

food_protection.q: During proper handwashing, hands must be rubbed together for at least:
choices: 5 seconds
10 seconds
20 seconds
30 seconds
food_protection.exp: Proper handwashing: wet hands, apply soap, lather up to elbows, use a nailbrush, rub for at least 20 seconds, rinse thoroughly, dry with disposable paper towels or air dryer. Never use cloth towels.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Personal Hygiene

food_protection.q: Which jewelry is PERMITTED for food workers during food preparation?
choices: Necklaces
Bracelets
Wedding band (plain ring)
Earrings
food_protection.exp: All jewelry poses a physical hazard and should not be worn by food workers — EXCEPT a plain wedding band, which is the one permitted exception under NYC Health Code.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Personal Hygiene

food_protection.q: Hand-washing sinks must be located within how many feet of each food preparation and service area?
choices: 10 feet
15 feet
25 feet
50 feet
food_protection.exp: NYC Health Code requires hand-washing sinks to be located within 25 feet of each food preparation, food service, and ware-washing area, and in or adjacent to employee and patron bathrooms.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Personal Hygiene

food_protection.q: A food worker with an infected cut or wound on their hand should:
choices: Wash the wound thoroughly and continue working
Wear double gloves to cover the wound
Be excluded from working with food until the wound heals
Cover the wound with a waterproof bandage and use single-use gloves or finger cots
food_protection.exp: All cuts and wounds that are not infected must be covered with a waterproof bandage. Wear single-use gloves or finger cots over bandages on hands or fingers. Infected wounds require exclusion from food handling.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Personal Hygiene

food_protection.q: Which of the following illnesses requires EXCLUSION of the food worker from the establishment?
choices: A mild headache
A common cold with no fever
Hepatitis A
A sprained wrist
food_protection.exp: Food workers with transmissible illnesses must be excluded: Hepatitis A, E. coli O157:H7, Salmonellosis, Shigellosis, Amebiasis, Cholera, Typhoid, Norovirus, and others. Exclusion applies until the worker is fully recovered.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Personal Hygiene

food_protection.q: Hand sanitizer can be used as a SUBSTITUTE for proper handwashing in a food establishment.
choices: True — hand sanitizer is more effective than soap and water
True — as long as it is at least 62% alcohol
False — hand sanitizer may be used IN ADDITION to handwashing, not instead of it
False — hand sanitizer is not permitted in food establishments
food_protection.exp: FALSE. Hand sanitizer can be used in ADDITION to handwashing but NEVER as a substitute. The high fat content on food workers' hands reduces sanitizer effectiveness — soap and water are essential.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Personal Hygiene

food_protection.q: Roughly 50–75% of healthy humans carry which bacterium in their nasal passages, which can contaminate food?
choices: E. coli
Staphylococcus aureus
Listeria monocytogenes
Clostridium perfringens
food_protection.exp: About 50–75% of healthy humans carry Staphylococcus aureus in their nasal passages. This can be transferred to food by touching the nose or blowing it. Staph is also found on infected cuts and skin.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Personal Hygiene

food_protection.q: Which thawing method is NOT permitted by NYC Health Code?
choices: In a refrigerator 24–48 hours before use
Under cold running water
In a microwave oven (if cooked immediately)
On the kitchen counter overnight at room temperature
food_protection.exp: Thawing at room temperature is NOT permitted. Acceptable methods: (1) refrigerator, (2) cold running water, (3) microwave oven (if immediately cooked). Room temperature thawing allows the outer surface to enter the danger zone.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Preparation

food_protection.q: Ready-to-eat foods may NOT be touched with bare hands because:
choices: The Health Code only permits gloves as a convenience
Bare hand contact always causes illness
Even with handwashing, not all contamination can be removed from hands
Ready-to-eat foods are always allergen-free
food_protection.exp: Proper handwashing reduces contamination but never removes it entirely. This is why the NYC Health Code prohibits bare hand contact with ready-to-eat foods — utensils, gloves, or deli paper must be used.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Preparation

food_protection.q: Cross contamination from raw to cooked foods can be prevented by all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: Storing raw meats below cooked foods in the refrigerator
Using dedicated food-washing sinks for raw meat and poultry
Washing hands between handling raw and cooked foods
Using the same cutting board for raw chicken and fresh salad vegetables
food_protection.exp: Using the same cutting board for raw chicken and fresh salad is a prime example of cross contamination. All other options are correct prevention measures. Dedicated, color-coded cutting boards are best practice.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Food Preparation

food_protection.q: The minimum internal cooking temperature for poultry, stuffed meats, and stuffing is:
choices: 140°F for 15 seconds
145°F for 15 seconds
158°F for 15 seconds
165°F for 15 seconds
food_protection.exp: Poultry (chicken, turkey, duck), stuffed meats, and stuffing containing meat must reach 165°F for at least 15 seconds. Stuffing acts as insulation and must be cooked separately from the poultry.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cooking Temperatures

food_protection.q: Ground meats and foods containing ground meats must be cooked to:
choices: 140°F for 15 seconds
145°F for 15 seconds
158°F for 15 seconds
165°F for 15 seconds
food_protection.exp: Ground meats must reach 158°F for 15 seconds. When meat is ground, surface microorganisms are mixed throughout the product — the internal temperature throughout must reach 158°F, not just the surface.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cooking Temperatures

food_protection.q: To destroy Trichinella, pork and foods containing pork must be cooked to:
choices: 150°F for 15 seconds
food_protection.exp: Pork must reach 150°F for at least 15 seconds to destroy Trichinella spiralis, Toxoplasma gondii, and other pork-associated microorganisms.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cooking Temperatures

food_protection.q: All other meats (lamb, goat, lobster, clams, oysters, mussels) and fish must be cooked to:
choices: 140°F for 15 seconds
food_protection.exp: All other meats and fish (lobster, shrimp, clams, oysters, mussels, lamb, goat, etc.) must reach 140°F for at least 15 seconds. Shell eggs also require 145°F unless the customer requests otherwise.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cooking Temperatures

food_protection.q: When using a microwave oven to cook PHF, you must add ___°F to the final required cooking temperature and allow food to stand covered for 2 minutes afterward.
food_protection.exp: Microwave cooking is uneven, creating cold spots. Add 25°F to the final required temperature when cooking in a microwave, and allow food to stand covered for 2 minutes to allow even heat distribution.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cooking Temperatures

food_protection.q: Hot holding units must maintain potentially hazardous foods at:
choices: 130°F or above
140°F or above
150°F or above
165°F or above
food_protection.exp: Foods held in hot holding units must be maintained at 140°F or above at all times. Only hot foods (already at 140°F) should be placed in hot holding units — never use them to reheat cold foods.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hot Holding, Cooling & Reheating

food_protection.q: NYC Health Code requires cooked foods to be cooled from 140°F to 70°F within:
choices: 30 minutes
1 hour
2 hours
4 hours
food_protection.exp: The rapid cooling requirement: 140°F → 70°F within 2 hours; then 70°F → 41°F within an additional 4 hours (total 6 hours). If food does not reach 70°F within 2 hours, it must be discarded.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hot Holding, Cooling & Reheating

food_protection.q: Foods that have not been cooled properly (140°F → 70°F in more than 2 hours, or 70°F → 41°F in more than 4 hours) should be:
choices: Reheated to 165°F and used within 2 hours
Chilled immediately in an ice bath
Considered contaminated and discarded
Served immediately before further spoilage
food_protection.exp: Foods that have been allowed to cool too slowly are considered contaminated. Even if reheated to 165°F, the toxins produced by bacteria during improper cooling (e.g., Staph toxins) are heat-stable and remain in the food.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hot Holding, Cooling & Reheating

food_protection.q: Effective methods to cool food rapidly include all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: Placing food in shallow pans (1–2 inches deep)
Cutting large pieces of meat into portions 6 lbs or less
Using an ice water bath while stirring frequently
Covering food with a tight lid in a large deep pot
food_protection.exp: Covering food in a large, deep pot slows cooling significantly. Correct methods: shallow pans, small portions, ice water baths, ice paddles, blast chillers. Food must be left UNCOVERED during cooling.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hot Holding, Cooling & Reheating

food_protection.q: Previously cooked and refrigerated food that will be served from a hot holding unit must be reheated to:
choices: 140°F within 2 hours
150°F for 15 seconds
165°F within 2 hours using a stove or oven
165°F — any method including hot holding unit
food_protection.exp: Reheating: previously cooked, cooled, and refrigerated PHF must be reheated to 165°F within 2 hours using a stove or oven. Hot holding units must NOT be used to reheat unless specifically designed for reheating.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Hot Holding, Cooling & Reheating

food_protection.q: What is the difference between CLEANING and SANITIZING?
choices: They are the same process with different names
Cleaning removes visible contamination; sanitizing reduces microorganisms to safe levels
Sanitizing removes visible dirt; cleaning kills bacteria
Cleaning applies to floors; sanitizing applies to food contact surfaces only
food_protection.exp: Cleaning removes visible contamination (soil, grease, food particles). Sanitizing reduces harmful microorganisms to an acceptable level. Both steps are required — you must clean BEFORE sanitizing.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cleaning & Sanitizing

food_protection.q: In the 3-compartment sink process, the CORRECT order is:
choices: Rinse → Wash → Sanitize → Air Dry
Wash → Sanitize → Rinse → Air Dry
Scrape/Pre-rinse → Wash → Rinse → Sanitize → Air Dry
Sanitize → Wash → Rinse → Air Dry
food_protection.exp: Correct 3-compartment sink order: (1) Scrape and pre-rinse, (2) Wash in first compartment (warm water + detergent, 110–120°F), (3) Rinse in second compartment (clean warm water), (4) Sanitize in third compartment, (5) Air dry.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cleaning & Sanitizing

food_protection.q: Hot water sanitizing in a 3-compartment sink requires the water temperature to be at least:
choices: 170°F for at least 30 seconds
food_protection.exp: Hot water sanitizing: utensils are soaked at 170°F for at least 30 seconds. The third compartment should be maintained at 170°F with a gas burner or electrical coils. A thermometer must verify the temperature.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cleaning & Sanitizing

food_protection.q: The most common and popular chemical sanitizer used in the food industry is:
choices: Iodine
Quaternary ammonium compounds
Chlorine (bleach)
Hydrogen peroxide
food_protection.exp: Chlorine (household bleach, 5.25% sodium hypochlorite) is the most widely used chemical sanitizer in the food industry. For immersion: 50 PPM at 75–140°F for 1 minute. For swabbing/spraying: 100 PPM.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cleaning & Sanitizing

food_protection.q: To make a 50 PPM chlorine sanitizing solution (for immersion), you need:
choices: 1/4 oz of bleach per gallon of water
1/2 oz of bleach per gallon of water
1 oz of bleach per gallon of water
2 oz of bleach per gallon of water
food_protection.exp: 50 PPM chlorine solution: 1/2 oz bleach per gallon of water. 100 PPM: 1 oz per gallon. 200 PPM: 2 oz per gallon. Use regular household bleach (5.25% sodium hypochlorite) — NOT ultra bleach or extra strength bleach.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cleaning & Sanitizing

food_protection.q: Wiping cloths used for cleaning food contact surfaces should be stored:
choices: In a dry container between uses
In a bucket of sanitizing solution of at least 50 PPM chlorine when not in use
Hanging near the food preparation area
In a clean, dry drawer
food_protection.exp: Wiping cloths must be stored in a bucket of sanitizing solution (at least 50 PPM) when not in use. The sanitizing solution must be changed frequently since food debris quickly depletes the sanitizer.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Cleaning & Sanitizing

food_protection.q: HACCP stands for:
choices: Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points
Health and Cleanliness Control Program
Hazard Assessment and Contamination Control Procedures
Health Academy Certification and Compliance Program
food_protection.exp: HACCP = Hazard Analysis Critical Control Points. Originally developed by Pillsbury Company for NASA's space program in the 1960s. It identifies and controls hazards throughout the food preparation process.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — HACCP

food_protection.q: A Critical Control Point (CCP) in HACCP is:
choices: Any step in the food flow where a hazard exists
A step at which action MUST be taken to prevent, reduce, or eliminate a hazard
A record-keeping requirement for food safety
The final inspection before food is served
food_protection.exp: A CCP is the step in the food production process where a critical limit must be met. Failure to meet the critical limit at a CCP will result in food that is unfit for consumption. Cooking temperature is a classic CCP.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — HACCP

food_protection.q: Which of the following is the CORRECT sequence of HACCP principles?
choices: Monitor → Identify hazards → Determine CCPs → Set limits → Verify → Correct → Record
Identify hazards → Determine CCPs → Set critical limits → Monitor → Correct → Verify → Record
Set limits → Identify hazards → Monitor → Determine CCPs → Correct → Verify → Record
Record → Verify → Identify hazards → Monitor → Determine CCPs → Correct → Set limits
food_protection.exp: 7 HACCP steps: (1) Identify hazards, (2) Determine CCPs, (3) Set critical limits, (4) Monitor CCPs, (5) Take corrective action, (6) Verify system is working, (7) Record keeping.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — HACCP

food_protection.q: In the Food Flow Diagram for fried chicken, which step is identified as a Critical Control Point (CCP)?
choices: Receiving from approved supplier
Storage at 41°F or below
Cooking to 165°F
Purchasing from a reputable vendor
food_protection.exp: Cooking is the CCP in the fried chicken food flow because it is the step where the temperature must reach 165°F to destroy Salmonella. Receiving and storage steps are important but are not CCPs in this flow.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — HACCP

food_protection.q: Which pest is the MOST serious threat to food safety in a restaurant due to its ability to carry and transfer pathogens?
choices: Fruit flies
German cockroaches
House flies
Mice
food_protection.exp: The house fly poses the gravest risk to food safety. It carries up to 28 million bacteria externally and 5 million internally, feeds on filth, and can contaminate any surface it lands on. Several species are classified as pathogenic flies.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Pest Control

food_protection.q: 'Fly zappers' (electric grid fly traps) are PROHIBITED in food establishments because:
choices: They are too expensive to operate
When flies are electrocuted, fly parts become airborne and can contaminate food
They attract more flies than they kill
They interfere with kitchen equipment
food_protection.exp: Fly zappers electrocute flies, causing fly parts to become airborne and fall on exposed food and food preparation areas. 'Fly catchers' (glue traps) are permitted because they stun and trap flies without dispersing them.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Pest Control

food_protection.q: Poisonous fly baits and pesticides in a food establishment:
choices: Can be used by any trained kitchen manager
Must be clearly labeled and can be applied by any employee
Can ONLY be used by a licensed pest control officer
Are prohibited under any circumstances
food_protection.exp: Poisonous baits and pesticide use inside food establishments require a licensed pest control officer (PCO). All food and ingredients must be removed or covered before application, and food contact surfaces must be cleaned afterward.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Pest Control

food_protection.q: German cockroaches (the most common restaurant pest) are typically found during the day:
choices: Flying around overhead lighting
In open food containers
Hiding behind baseboard moldings, in cracks around cabinets, under stoves, and near water sources
Around exterior garbage areas
food_protection.exp: German cockroaches prefer to be very near food and water. During the day they hide behind baseboard moldings, in cracks around cabinets, drawers, pantries, under stoves, inside microwave ovens, and near refrigerators.
food_protection.source: NYC Food Protection Certificate — Pest Control
